coredata.dc:title: Killing two birds with one stone: Construction of a rare earth hybrid dual-channel fluorescent biosensor with intelligent broadcasting function and visualized synchronous assessment of multi-objectives
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Jun
Li, Rui
Xu, Zhitao
Chen, Xiangzhen
Li, Yongxin
Zhao, Tongqian
Jia, Lei
coredata.dc:description: Contamination of pathogenic bacteria and abuse of antibiotics have become important factors restricting the healthy and rapid development of animal husbandry. Simultaneous quantitative detection of pathogenic bacteria and antibiotics is not only the need of animal husbandry development, but also a challenge in the development of sensor analysis technology. In this study, a dual-channel fluorescent biosensor based on tubular halloysite (THT) loaded with carbon dots and lanthanide bimetal organic frameworks was prepared (THT@CDs-MOF), which contained blue emitters (peak emission at 450 nm under 365 nm excitation) and red emitters (peak emission at 616 nm under 254 nm excitation). The obtained dual-channel biosensor can realize quick visual intelligent assessment of 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) through energy transfer under 254 nm excitation and tetracycline (TC) through antenna effect and internal filtering effect (IFE) under 365 nm excitation. This biosensor can detect DPA with a concentration of 0–72 μM (LOD was 6.07 nM) and TC with a concentration of 0–19 μM (LOD was 11.31 nM). By fixing the biosensor on agarose, a biosensor with rapid visualization for simultaneous detection of DPA and TC was obtained. Importantly, a color recognition speech broadcasting system based on 51 single-chip microcomputer for the real-time semi-quantitative identification of DPA and TC was also developed, which promoted the application of artificial intelligence in the field of small molecules analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-channel
Rare earth
Visualized assessment
Intelligent broadcasting
6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid
Tetracycline

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Dual-channel fluorescence sensing mechanism of THT@CDs-MOF
Sensing mechanism of THT@CDs-MOF for DPA
Sensing mechanism of THT@CDs-MOF for TC
Optimal experimental conditions for TC and DPA recognition
Multicolor sensing of THT@CDs-MOF to TC
Multicolor sensing of THT@CDs-MOF to DPA
Selectivity and anti-interference of THT@CDs-MOF for TC and DPA assessment
Analysis of TC and DPA in practical samples by THT@CDs-MOF
Dual-channel visual analysis based on agarose gelled detection device
Application of THT@CDs-MOF biosensor in intelligent electronic sensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was partly supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U1904168 , No. U1704241 and No. 51773052 ), the Science Fund for Excellent Young Scholars of Henan Province ( 222300420047 ), the Plan for Scientific Innovation Talent of Henan Province ( 194200510010 ), the Program for Science & Technology Innovation Talents in Universities of Henan Province (No. 20HASTIT015 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images and particle size distribution of THT (inset are the width distribution and length distribution) (a), TEM image of carbon dots (inset are the particle size distribution and high resolution TEM image) (b), TEM images of THT@CDs (c), HRTEM image of THT@CDs (d and e), TEM image of THT@CDs-MOF (f), SEM images of THT(g) and THT@CDs-MOF (h).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of THT@CDs-MOF before and after adding DPA (5 μM), (b) Energy transfer mechanism diagram of THT@CDs-MOF for DPA recognition, (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of THT@CDs-MOF before and after adding TC (5 μM), (d) Energy transfer mechanism diagram of TC recognition by THT@CDs-MOF, (e) Fluorescence lifetime curves of THT@CDs-MOF and THT@CDs-MOF+TC in H2O and D2O, (f) UV absorption spectra of THT@CDs, THT@CDs+TC, THT@CDs-MOF, THT@CDs-MOF+TC and TC, (g) Fluorescence emission spectra of THT@CDs and UV absorption spectra of TC, (h) Fluorescence lifetime curves of THT@CDs before and after adding TC (10 and 30 μM).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of THT@CDs-MOF at different TC concentrations (0–19 μM), (b) the linear relationship between I616/I450 ratios and TC concentrations in the range of 0–6 μM and 6–19 μM, (c) CIE chromaticity coordinates of THT@CDs-MOF response to different concentrations of TC, (d) Photos of fluorescent colorimetric dishes with different TC contents excited at 365 nm.

Fluorescence spectra of THT@CDs-MOF at different DPA concentrations (0–3.5 μM) (a) and (0–72 μM) (b), (c) Photos of fluorescent colorimetric dishes with different DPA under 254 nm UV lamp, (d) the linear relationship between I545/I616 ratios and DPA concentrations.
Preparation of visualized detection device based on agarose and application diagram of intelligent detection of DPA and TC.
Detection of DPA (a) and TC (b) by a color recognition speech broadcasting system based on 51 single-chip microcomputer.
The preparation diagrammatic drawing of dual-channel multicolor fluorescent nanoprobe THT@CDs-MOF and the sensing schematics of DPA and TC.

head.ce:title: Killing two birds with one stone: Construction of a rare earth hybrid dual-channel fluorescent biosensor with intelligent broadcasting function and visualized synchronous assessment of multi-objectives

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo, Henan 454000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo, Henan, 454000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Resources & Environment, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo, Henan 454000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Resources & Environment, Henan Polytechnic University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Resources & Environment, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo, Henan, 454000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Contamination of pathogenic bacteria and abuse of antibiotics have become important factors restricting the healthy and rapid development of animal husbandry. Simultaneous quantitative detection of pathogenic bacteria and antibiotics is not only the need of animal husbandry development, but also a challenge in the development of sensor analysis technology. In this study, a dual-channel fluorescent biosensor based on tubular halloysite (THT) loaded with carbon dots and lanthanide bimetal organic frameworks was prepared (THT@CDs-MOF), which contained blue emitters (peak emission at 450 nm under 365 nm excitation) and red emitters (peak emission at 616 nm under 254 nm excitation). The obtained dual-channel biosensor can realize quick visual intelligent assessment of 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) through energy transfer under 254 nm excitation and tetracycline (TC) through antenna effect and internal filtering effect (IFE) under 365 nm excitation. This biosensor can detect DPA with a concentration of 0–72 μM (LOD was 6.07 nM) and TC with a concentration of 0–19 μM (LOD was 11.31 nM). By fixing the biosensor on agarose, a biosensor with rapid visualization for simultaneous detection of DPA and TC was obtained. Importantly, a color recognition speech broadcasting system based on 51 single-chip microcomputer for the real-time semi-quantitative identification of DPA and TC was also developed, which promoted the application of artificial intelligence in the field of small molecules analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, environmental pollution caused by the accelerating industrial development and water pollution induced by the spread of microorganisms have become increasingly serious, which greatly affects people’s daily life and health [1]. Tetracycline (TC) antibiotics are multifunctional broad-spectrum antibiotics that can inhibit bacterial protein synthesis and are widely used in the treatment of bacterial infections in humans and animals [2]. However, a large number of TC antibiotics remain in food and agricultural production, and they are finally damaged by human intake. Therefore, rapid, sensitive, and efficient assessment of tetracycline residues is crucial. The spread of microorganisms also plays a great role in the outbreak of diseases and biological terrorist attacks. Bacterial spores still have strong vitality under harsh conditions, and they can still be revived in a suitable environment even after their activity is destroyed [3,4]. The spores of Bacillus anthracis are dangerous bacteria that cause anthrax. They infect animals and humans through feeding, breathing, and skin contact, which causes serious damage to the skin, intestines, and lungs of humans and animals; this condition ultimately leads to anthrax infectious diseases. Inhalation of Bacillus anthracis can lead to dyspnea and a high mortality rate of over 90% within 36 h [5]. 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) is one of the main biomarkers of Bacillus anthracis, which accounts for about 5–15% of spore dry weight [6]. Therefore, the development of fast and on-site identification method of anthracnose spores is important in reducing disease outbreaks and preventing biological terrorist attacks [7]. And simultaneous quantitative detection of pathogenic bacteria and antibiotics is not only the need of animal husbandry development, but also a challenge in the development of sensor analysis technology.

Various analytical methods have been developed to detect TC and DPA in recent decades. Traditional TC detection methods often include high-performance liquid chromatography [8], capillary electrophoresis [9], and liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry [10]. Although the sensitivity and accuracy of these methods are very high, the instruments are costly and time consuming, sample pretreatment is cumbersome, and the testers with professional skills are needed. The detection of DPA includes polymerase chain reaction and immunoassay, but it always requires long cycles and complex sample operations [11–13].

Compared with the abovementioned detection methods, fluorescence analysis method can rapidly, sensitively, and selectively realize the fast analysis of the analytes and has low detection limit; thus, it is considered to be a powerful detection method [14–19]. The traditional single emission fluorescence method can only use the enhancement or weakening of a single fluorescence color to analyze [20]. Compared with the single emission fluorescent probe, the polychromatic fluorescence analysis method can eliminate the fluorescence intensity fluctuation caused by the single emission fluorescent probe through self-calibration [21–23]. At the same time, the ratiometric fluorescent probes with rich colors often show obvious color changes with the assistance of UV lamps, which provides the possibility for naked-eye visual sensing [23]. Although large numbers of methods to perform assays of DPA and TC using fluorescent detection methods have been reported [24–27], approaches for discriminating and synchronous detecting the two markers mentioned above are few.

Luminescent lanthanide metal–organic frameworks have unique optical properties, such as large Stokes shift, narrow emission band, and long fluorescence lifetime. With the fluorescence specificity of different lanthanide metals, fluorescence detection of environmental pollutants and biological molecules can be realized [28–32]. For example, lanthanide-doped probes for DPA fluorescence sensing generally exhibit characteristic fluorescence emission peaks under 254 nm excitation [30,33], while lanthanide hybrid fluorescent probes for TC detection usually exhibit characteristic fluorescence under 365 nm excitation [31,32]. Inspired by our previous research and the abovementioned research background and considering whether the rare earth Eu3+ and Tb3+ are co-modified in the same material, the potential fluorescence recognition sites of different analytes can be constructed under 254 nm and 365 nm channels, respectively. It is expected to realize the dual-channel distinguishing detection of DPA and TC and expand the application of rare earth hybrid fluorescent probes.

On the basis of the above analysis, tubular halloysite (THT) with good water dispersibility and surface modification characteristics was used in this study to introduce fluorescent carbon dots and rare earth fluorescent MOF on its surface by in-situ and hydrothermal synthesis, respectively. As shown in Scheme 1, the dual-channel biosensor can achieve the specific visual sensing of DPA and TC under different excitation channels. The perfect combination of natural porous materials THT with bimetallic rare earth organic frameworks and carbon dots effectively improved the stability and detection sensitivity of fluorescent probes, which was useful for fast, effective, and intuitive assessment of biomarkers. Furthermore, a dual-channel fluorescence biosensor with rapid visualized assessment of DPA and TC was obtained by fixing the biosensor on agarose gel. More importantly, a color recognition speech broadcasting system based on 51 single-chip microcomputer for the real-time semi-quantitative identification of DPA and TC was also developed, which promoted the application of artificial intelligence in the field of small molecules analysis.

All in materials, characterization, the detailed synthetic steps, the detailed detection methods, the preparation of the agarose gelled multicolor biosensor, and the design and application of color recognition speech broadcasting system based on 51 single-chip microcomputer were listed in the Supporting Information.

The morphology of THT before and after functional modification was characterized by TEM and SEM. It can be seen from Fig. 1a and b that the average length and width of THT were 0.71 µm and 54.63 nm, respectively. The average particle size of CDs was 2.87 nm, and the lattice spacing was 0.24 nm. Fig. 1c and d indicated that surface of THT became rough after CDs were uniformly loaded on the surface of THT. From HRTEM (Fig. 1e and b), it can be observed that the lattice spacing of in-situ grown CDs was maintained at 0.24 nm, which proved that the morphology and structure of CDs did not change before and after loading. When the surface of THT @ CDs was loaded with MOF, it can be seen from Fig. 1f that a large number of rare earth MOFs were loaded around the nanotubes. EDX mapping in Fig. S1 clearly shows the element distributions in THT@CDs-MOF. Since the atomic mass of rare earth elements was much larger than that of elements contained in THT@CDs, the bright part of HAADF-STEM image in Fig. S1a was rare earth elements, and the dark parts were other elements. The EDX-mapping diagram of Figs. S1b-1f fully proved that rare earth MOFs were perfectly loaded around THT@CDs nanotubes. The SEM images shown in Fig. 1g and h also displayed that the surface of THT changed from smooth to rough after the modification, which also demonstrated the successful construction of THT@CDs-MOF composited biosensor. Furthermore, THT and THT@CDs were dispersed in buffer and the fluorescent properties were investigated to further prove that the CDs shown in Fig. S2a were indeed in situ loaded on the surface of THT. Fig. S2b shows that fully washed THT@CDs sample displayed bright blue fluorescence, which implied that the blue fluorescence in the composited biosensor was attributed to the fluorescence of carbon dots. As shown in Fig. S2c, the existing of N, Si, O, Tb, and Eu elements indicated that MOF was successfully introduced to the surface of THT@CDs.

The microstructures of THT, THT@CDs, MOF, and THT@CDs-MOF were further analyzed by XRD. As shown in Fig. S3a, the strong typical characteristic peaks of THT at 12.22°, 19.85°, 24.79°, 34.98° and 38.45° were clearly observed in THT, THT@CDs, and THT@CDs-MOF. These peaks corresponded to the (001), (020), (002), (130), and (132) facets of THT (JCPDS # 29–1487) [34–36], respectively, which meant that the structure of THT carrier of the composited biosensor remained unchanged before and after modification. However, after rare earth MOF was loaded, comparison of the diffraction patterns of THT and THT@CDs-MOF showed that the typical characteristic absorption of MOF appeared on THT@CDs-MOF [37].

The surface functional group properties of each samples were investigated by FTIR spectroscopy. As displayed in Fig. S3b, the absorptions of THT at 3706, 3626, and 907 cm−1 were attributed to the strong Si–OH stretching vibration peak in THT [38]. The peak at 1026 cm−1 was caused by the Si–O–Si skeleton vibration of THT [32]. From the infrared spectrum of THT@CDs, a new peak appeared at 1650 cm−1, which can be assigned to the stretching vibration of CO in citric acid; this phenomenon indicated that CDs were successfully loaded in THT [39]. The infrared characteristic peaks of MOF at 1375 and 1433 cm−1 can be attributed to the C–O functional group of Eu/Tb MOF chelate, and characteristic peaks at 1558 and 1612 cm−1 can be attributed to the CO functional group of Eu/Tb MOF chelate [40,41]. All of the related characteristic peaks of THT, THT@CDs, and MOF can be observed in the infrared spectrum of THT@CDs-MOF, which proved the successful preparation of the biosensor.

The full scanning XPS spectrum illustrated in Fig. S4a indicated the peaks of binding energy at 74.53, 102.08, 285.51, 400.24, and 531.5 eV corresponded to Al 2p, Si 2p, C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively. Fig. S5a demonstrated that the binding energies at 1135.65, 1166.43, 1280.29, and 1243.9 eV were attributed to the representative peaks of Eu 3d and Tb 3d, which meant that MOF materials were successfully wrapped on the surface of THT@CDs. As shown in Fig. S5b, the specific peaks at 284.5, 285.16, 286.2, 287.92, and 288.7 eV corresponded to the C–C, C–N, C = C, C–O, and CO bonds, respectively [42]. The O1s spectrum in Fig. S5c indicated that the three main peaks at 531.4, 532.11, and 532.55 eV were assigned to O element in the form of OC, O–C, and O–Si [43,44]. The characteristic peaks of 399.42, 400.33, and 401.7 eV in N1s spectra shown in Fig. S5d confirmed the existence of N–C, N–O, and N–H bonds. As shown in Fig. S4b, four peaks appeared in the multiple splittings of Eu spin orbit (j = 3/2 and 5/2 energies) after grafting Eu/Tb-MOF. The peaks at 1125.8 and 1155.55 eV belonged to Eu2+3d5/2 and Eu2+3d3/2, and those at 1135.7 and 1165.82 eV belonged to Eu3+3d5/2 and Eu3+3d3/2 [45]. As shown in Fig. S4c, the peaks at 1241.9 and 1276.8 eV in the Tb 3d energy spectrum corresponded to the valence state of Tb3+ [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-channel fluorescence sensing mechanism of THT@CDs-MOF
Sensing mechanism of THT@CDs-MOF for DPA
ce:section.ce:para: The luminescent properties of THT@CDs-MOF and the energy transfer process between lanthanide metals are shown in Fig. 2a and b. When no DPA was added, due to the effective energy transfer from Tb3+ to Eu3+ in THT@CDs-MOF biosensor [37], the red emission dominated in Fig. 2a (black). However, the emission spectrum of the biosensor changed after the addition of DPA. The lowest triple levels of DPA allowed energy transfer to two lanthanide ions. Compared with Eu3+, the energy transfer from DPA ligand to Tb3+ ions was dominant, and DPA can replace the coordinated water molecules and chelate with Tb3+ to form [Tb(DPA)]+ complexes [47]. Therefore, the presence of DPA significantly reduced the energy transfer from Tb3+ to Eu3+, and the fluorescence color of the bimetallic THT@CDs-MOF sensor gradually changed from red to green.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of THT@CDs-MOF for TC
ce:section.ce:para:
As illustrated in Fig. 2c, the biosensor only showed the blue fluorescence of CDs without TC. However, the emission spectra of THT@CDs-MOF with 5 μM TC showed four typical characteristic emissions of Eu3+ at 593, 616, 652, and 695 nm, respectively [32]. This phenomenon was due to the transformation of energy from excited state (5d0) to ground state (7fj, J=1, 2, 3, 4), and the transformation from 5d0 to 7f2 was more obvious (Fig. 2d). Owing to the antenna effect [48], the unique ketone carbonyl group in β-diketone molecule can coordinate with Eu ions, and the resulting energy transfer can sensitize Eu ions to exhibit intrinsic red fluorescence emission. TC can also effectively inhibit the non-radiative quenching of water after the coordinated H2O molecules near Eu3+ were replaced [32,48]. The variation of fluorescence lifetimes of the THT@CDs-MOF biosensor in H2O and D2O were determined and the double exponential function models (Formulas 1 and 2) were fitted to calculate the average fluorescence lifetime of the biosensor, which are shown in Fig. 2e. The coordination numbers of H2O were calculated according to Formula (3) established by Horrocks and Supkowski. The data listed in Table S1 indicated that TC replaced five water molecules after 5 μM TC was added, which demonstrated that Eu ions were more likely to coordinate with TC than water molecules.

The sensing mechanism between fluorescent probes and detected substances can usually be divided into internal filtering effect (IFE), dynamic quenching effect, and static quenching effect [49]. Firstly, the UV–visible spectra of THT@CDs, TC, and THT@CDs-TC in Fig. 2f showed that there was no new absorption peak was observed, which eliminated the static quenching. Secondly, the fluorescence excitation spectra of THT@CDs overlapped with the absorption spectra of TC, as shown in Fig. 2g. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching may be caused by IFE or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [49]. However, Fig. 2h and Table S2 indicated the fluorescence lifetime of THT@CDs was nearly unchanged after TC addition, which eliminated the FRET effect. In summary, the fluorescence quenching of THT@CDs biosensor after the introduction of TC was due to the IFE [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimal experimental conditions for TC and DPA recognition
ce:section.ce:para: The changes in emission peak and fluorescence intensity of THT@CDs at different excitation bands were shown in Fig. S6, which meant that THT@CDs can emit blue light only near the excitation wavelength of 365 nm, and no blue light was emitted near the excitation wavelength of 254 nm. After the addition of 6 μM TC or DPA, the results in Fig. S7 showed that the optimal excitation for TC and DPA detection was 375 and 280 nm. The variation in fluorescence intensities under different pH conditions after adding a certain amount of TC or DPA was investigated. Figs. S8a and 8b illustrate that the fluorescence intensity ratios of I616/I450 and I545/I616 displayed certain regular changes with the variation in pH, which indicated that the optimal detection pH values of TC and DPA were 9 and 8, respectively [50]. Figs. S8c and S8d show that the fluorescence ratios of THT@CDs-MOF with 6 μM TC and DPA at I616/I450 and I545/I616 reached equilibrium within 15 and 25 s, respectively, which indicated the rare earth ions had strong chelating effect on the two analytes mentioned above. The fluorescence stabilities of THT@CDs-MOF before and after the addition of TC and DPA under xenon lamp high-voltage illumination for 800 s illustrated in Figs. S8e and S8f proved THT@CDs-MOF had good photostability and anti-bleaching performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multicolor sensing of THT@CDs-MOF to TC
ce:section.ce:para: Under the aforementioned optimal conditions, the sensitivity of THT@CDs-MOF was detected. With the increase in TC, the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm decreased slightly, and the red fluorescence intensity at 616 nm increased significantly, as shown in Fig. 3a. In the range of 0–6 μM and 6–19 μM, the concentration of TC illustrated a good linear relationship with the fluorescence intensity, and the correlation coefficients were 0.9976 and 0.9804, respectively. The linear equations can be expressed as I616/I450 = 0.2807 CTC+ 0.1414 and I616/I450 = 0.0794 CTC+ 1.3123, as shown in Fig. 3b. The detection limit was 11.31 nM, which was far lower than the maximum residue limits (0.676 and 0.225 μM) of TC in milk [51]. The emission spectra and CIE coordinates of TC at different concentrations and the coordinates corresponding to different concentrations are shown in Fig. 3c and Table S3. After the addition of 0–6 μM TC, the visualized images (Fig. 3d) of the colorimetric dish photographed under 365 nm UV lamp indicated that the biosensor can achieve TC assessment through colorful variation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multicolor sensing of THT@CDs-MOF to DPA
ce:section.ce:para: With the increase in DPA content, the difference in the energy transfer efficiency of DPA to the two rare earth ions led to the most obvious enhancement in the fluorescence intensity of Tb3+. The fluorescence color of THT@CDs-MOF also gradually varied from red to green. Notably, as shown in Fig. 4a and b, the probe can still achieve DPA detection in the presence of low concentrations (0–3.5 μM) and high concentrations (0–72 μM) of DPA. As shown in Fig. 4c, the color of the fluorescent sensor changed dramatically in the presence of 0–3.5 μM DPA, which implied that THT@CDs-MOF can achieve the ultra-low assessment of DPA. The concentration of DPA illustrated a good linear relationship with the fluorescence intensity, as listed in Fig. 4d. The linear equation was I545/I616 = 3.5407 CDPA+ 0.4455, and the detection limit related to the correlation coefficient R= 0.9978 was as low as 6.07 nM, which was significantly lower than the minimum infection dosages (60 μM) [19,30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference of THT@CDs-MOF for TC and DPA assessment
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of THT@CDs-MOF on TC and DPA detection with the coexistence of various cations, anions and multiple antibiotics or biomarkers were further studied to verify the application of THT@CDs-MOF biosensor. Figs. S9 and S10 indicated the existence of the abovementioned anions, cations and multiple antibiotics or biomarkers had no effect on TC and DPA assessment, which meant that the fluorescent biosensor THT@CDs-MOF had good anti-interference ability. Furthermore, as the chelates of DPA-Tb and TC-Eu showed their respective fluorescence only under the excitation of 254 nm and 365 nm, respectively. Therefore, the coexistence of TC had little effect on the detection of DPA, and vice versa (Fig. S11).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of TC and DPA in practical samples by THT@CDs-MOF
ce:section.ce:para: Different concentrations of TC and DPA were added to common samples such as tap water, lake water, honey, and milk to determine the detecting performance of THT@CDs-MOF for TC and DPA in actual environmental samples. The reproducibility of the aforementioned actual samples was calculated by fluorescence titration experiments. As shown in Tables S4 and S5, the detection reproducibility rates of THT@CDs-MOF biosensor for TC and DPA were 94.05–106.61% and 93.92–108.44%, respectively, and the relative deviations (RSD, n = 3) were 1.43–4.37% and 1.21–2.98%, respectively. The results illustrated that the THT@CDs-MOF could still maintain good selectivity and sensitivity in common environmental samples, and it could become a high-reliability TC and DPA ratio luminescent sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-channel visual analysis based on agarose gelled detection device
ce:section.ce:para: A gelled fluorescent detection device was developed to further eliminate the background interference of glass devices such as colorimetric dishes in fluorescence sensing. First, agarose with no UV absorption and transparent surface was selected as the carrier. Then, THT@CDs-MOF was fixed in agarose to obtain gelled fluorescent sensors with different shapes. As illustrated in Fig. 5, the fluorescence color of agarose under 254 nm UV channel displayed a dramatic variation from red to green with continuously increase of DPA, and this phenomenon was easily captured by smart phone cameras. The green (G) and red (R) values were analyzed and calculated by color scanning APP [52–54]. The fitting curve Y= 0.3907 X0.3143 was obtained by setting X as the concentration of DPA and Y as the ratio of G/R. The assessment of TC in the 365 nm UV channel was similar to the abovementioned method. Through the analysis and calculation of blue (B) and red (R) values by color scanning APP, the fitting curve Y= 0.3557 X0.5354 can be obtained by setting X as the concentration of TC and Y as the ratio of B/R. The abovementioned results demonstrated that the agarose gelled detection device can achieve the on-site visualized dual-channel synchronous analysis of DPA and TC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of THT@CDs-MOF biosensor in intelligent electronic sensor
ce:section.ce:para: According to the color change phenomenon of THT@CDs-MOF caused by biological small molecules (DPA and TC), a color recognition speech broadcasting system based on 51 single-chip microcomputer was designed, circuit diagram was shown in Fig. S12. The electronic sensor can quickly determine the concentration range by identifying the fluorescence color of the gelled detection device, and it can intelligently broadcast the corresponding concentration of small biological molecules. The detection system was divided into voice broadcast module, liquid crystal display (LCD) module, and color recognition module. As shown in Fig. 6, color recognition module can accurately analyze the fluorescent color of THT@CDs-MOF biosensor obtained by smart phones under UV light. When the corresponding concentration and color information appeared on the display, the voice broadcast module will broadcast specific information in real time. The study of the material properties indicated that red fluorescence was found under 254 nm UV light irradiation when the concentration of DPA was 0–0.4 μmol/L, and other DPA and TC dosages corresponding to different colors can also be observed. Therefore, the color recognition module was used to identify the fluorescent color of the fluorescent biosensor captured by the smart phone after small biological molecules were added under the irradiation of UV light. At this time, the LCD displays will present the corresponding UV light band, fluorescent color, and the corresponding concentration range of the color. The voice broadcast module will automatically broadcast the corresponding color and concentration range in real time. For example, the color identified by the color recognizer was orange under the irradiation of 254 nm UV lamp. At this time, the LCD displays will show the information of “UV= 254 nm {Orange}, 0.4–2 μmol/ L,” and the speaker will broadcast “Orange, 0.4–2 μmol/L,” which is shown in the movie file in the supporting information. This method can minimize the error of human color recognition, and it was expected to be implanted into the robot in the later stage to truly realize the intelligent recognition of the measured objects.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132341.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Movie S1 and Movie S2.
Movie S1
Movie S2
.

This study prepared a hybrid dual-channel fluorescent biosensor made of tubular kaolin, fluorescent carbon dots, and lanthanide organic framework. This hybrid biosensor realized the naked-eye visual discrimination and recognition of DPA and TC in 254 and 365 nm channels, respectively. A portable gel detection device was also obtained by immobilizing the biosensor on agarose, which achieved the dual-channel discrimination detection of DPA and TC with the help of smart phones. Importantly, this study designed a color recognition speech broadcasting system based on 51 single-chip microcomputer. This electronic sensor can quickly determine the concentration range and intelligently broadcast the corresponding concentration of small biological molecules. This method can minimize the error of human color recognition, and this single-chip microcomputer would be implanted into the robot to truly realize the intelligent recognition of the measured objects.

Jun Xu: Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition, Review and editing, Supervision, Visualization. Rui Li: Methodology, Data curation, Review and editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision., mZhitao Xu: Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization., Xiangzhen Chen: Investigation, Formal analysis., Yongxin Li: Investigation, Review and editing, Formal analysis.Tongqian Zhao: Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Review and editing. Lei Jia: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Resources, Review and editing, Visualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partly supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U1904168, No. U1704241 and No. 51773052), the Science Fund for Excellent Young Scholars of Henan Province (222300420047), the Plan for Scientific Innovation Talent of Henan Province (194200510010), the Program for Science & Technology Innovation Talents in Universities of Henan Province (No. 20HASTIT015).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132341.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Chu, H. Wang, Y. Du, F. Yang, L. Yang, C. Jiang, A portable smartphone platform using ratiometric fluorescent paper strip for visual quantitative sensing, ACS Sustainable Chem. Eng. 8 (2020) 8175–8183.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jun Xu received her Ph. D. from Lanzhou University in 2013, Lanzhou, Gansu, China. Then she entered Postdoctoral Station of Geological Resources and Geological Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University in 2018. She worked as a faculty in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University. Her current research focus is luminescent functional lanthanide materials.
Rui Li is a M.S. student at Henan Polytechnic University. Her research interests include spectral analysis and biosensor.
Zhitao Xu is a M.S. student at Henan Polytechnic University. His research interests include spectral analysis and biosensor.
Xiangzhen Chen is a Ph. D. at Henan Polytechnic University. Her research interests include spectral analysis and biosensor.

Yongxin Li received her Ph. D. from Lanzhou University in 2021, Lanzhou, Gansu, China. She worked as a faculty in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University. Her current research focus is luminescent functional materials.

Tongqian Zhao received his Ph. D. from Research Center for Eco-Environmental Sciences in 2004, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, China. Then he entered postdoctoral station of environmental science and engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences in the same year. He worked as a full professor at Institute of Resources & Environment, Henan Polytechnic University. His current research focus is pollutant control and environmental engineering.

Lei Jia received his Ph. D. from Lanzhou University in 2010, Lanzhou, Gansu, China. He spent one year at the National University of Singapore for Ph. D. study. Then he entered postdoctoral station of State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, in 2011. He worked as a full professor at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University. His research interests and topics include luminescent functional materials and information storage and intelligent anti-counterfeiting materials
